card.title: How Wi-Fi Actually Works
card.teaser: Your router is basically a tiny radio station — and your laptop has been tuned in the whole time.
card.coverImagePrompt: A stylized cross-section of a living room with visible radio waves rippling out from a router, soft teal glow, devices quietly listening, cinematic illustration

lessons.title: It's just radio — but smarter
lessons.body: Wi-Fi isn't magic. It's the same kind of radio wave that carried FM stations in the 1950s, just tuned to much higher frequencies and switching direction thousands of times a second. Your router is a tiny transmitter. Your laptop is a tiny receiver. And the whole apartment building is screaming over each other on a handful of channels.

visual.imagePrompt: A vintage radio and a modern Wi-Fi router side by side, radio waves shown pouring from both in warm-yellow versus cool-cyan

lessons.title: Two-way conversation in invisible light
lessons.body: Wi-Fi uses electromagnetic waves — the same physics as visible light, but at frequencies your eyes can't see (usually 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz). Your router broadcasts tiny pulses encoding 1s and 0s. Your devices send pulses back. It's a two-way conversation happening millions of times faster than you can blink.

keyTerms.definition: How many wave cycles happen per second, measured in Hertz (Hz)
A device (usually your router) that bridges wireless devices to a wired network

visual.imagePrompt: An oscilloscope-style animation showing encoded binary pulses on a radio wave, glowing neon blue

lessons.title: 2.4 vs. 5 GHz: the classic tradeoff
lessons.body: The 2.4 GHz band travels farther and slips through walls more easily — but it's crowded. Microwaves, baby monitors, and your neighbor's router all share it. The 5 GHz band is faster and quieter but falls off quickly through walls. That's why your Wi-Fi can feel blazing fast next to the router and crawl in the back bedroom — you're switching bands whether you realize it or not.

visual.imagePrompt: Floor plan of an apartment showing two overlapping Wi-Fi coverage zones — 2.4 GHz wider and weaker, 5 GHz tighter and brighter

lessons.title: Why your microwave kills Netflix
lessons.body: A microwave oven heats food using the same 2.4 GHz frequency Wi-Fi uses. The oven is shielded — but not perfectly. When you hit 'popcorn,' some energy leaks out and drowns your router's signal like someone shouting over a whisper. In 1999, Wi-Fi's inventors chose 2.4 GHz precisely because it was unlicensed — the trade-off was sharing it with appliances.
lessons.funFact: Wi-Fi's backbone algorithm — how devices politely wait to avoid talking over each other — was inspired by aloha-net, a 1970s radio system used to network computers across Hawaiian islands.

visual.imagePrompt: A microwave oven glowing ominously as Wi-Fi bars nearby flicker and drop, playful but dramatic

lessons.title: A miracle hiding in plain sight
lessons.body: Right now, invisible waves carrying billions of bits are passing through your body. Wi-Fi took a 19th-century discovery — electromagnetic waves — and turned it into the silent nervous system of modern life. Next time it drops, remember: the real miracle isn't that it glitches sometimes. It's that it ever works at all.

quiz.question: What kind of signal does Wi-Fi use?
quiz.explanation: Wi-Fi uses radio-frequency electromagnetic waves, typically at 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz.

quiz.question: Why is 5 GHz Wi-Fi often faster but shorter-range than 2.4 GHz?
options: Higher frequency carries more data but travels shorter distances and penetrates walls less
It uses a different type of wave
It only works with newer routers
quiz.explanation: Higher-frequency waves can carry more data but are absorbed by walls and obstacles more easily.

quiz.question: Why can a microwave oven interfere with Wi-Fi?
options: It uses the same 2.4 GHz frequency, and some energy leaks out
quiz.explanation: Microwaves operate at 2.4 GHz, the same band many Wi-Fi networks use, and imperfect shielding leaks interference.
